perturbation.original_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a): the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 45 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal"). We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Legal Remedy' clause into the 'No Solicitation' section makes it less visible and could be misinterpreted as applying only to breaches related to solicitation, not the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. By burying the clause about specific performance and injunctive relief in a section about 'No Solicitation,' it obscures the broader applicability of these remedies, potentially hindering a party's ability to obtain such relief for breaches outside of the 'No Solicitation' context. The law expects equitable remedies to be readily apparent and applicable to the entire agreement, not hidden within specific clauses.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of relocating the 'Legal Remedy' clause into the 'No Solicitation' section creates a legal contradiction. This placement obscures the broader applicability of remedies like specific performance and injunctive relief, potentially hindering a party's ability to obtain such relief for breaches beyond the 'No Solicitation' context. The Law Explanation also supports this. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically CPLR § 6301, discusses the grounds for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. By burying the remedy clause within a specific section, it weakens the application of these remedies to the entire agreement, making it more difficult to enforce, which leads to a clear non-compliance with how easily remedies can be obtained.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidentiality of Evaluation Materials --------------------------------------- We will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its financial advisor, J.P. Morgan & Co. Incorporated ("JPM"), or the Company's other representatives furnish to us in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by us, or by our representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or our review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials"). This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to the 'Confidentiality' section makes it easily missed when looking for legal terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law to govern contracts. However, it implicitly assumes this choice is made clear and conspicuous. Placing the governing law clause within the Confidentiality section makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially leading to disputes about which law applies. A clearly defined and easily located governing law clause is crucial for enforceability and predictability, something undermined by this relocation.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that the relocation of the governing law clause within the 'Confidentiality' section makes it easily missed, undermining its prominence. The Law Explanation supports this by arguing that a clearly defined and easily located governing law clause is crucial for enforceability, which is undermined by its relocation. NY GOL § 5-1401, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, allows parties to choose New York law to govern contracts. While it doesn't explicitly state that the clause must be in a prominent location, the placement within a confidentiality section creates a structural flaw (as defined), potentially leading to disputes about which law applies, contradicting the intent for the governing law to be clearly established. This constitutes a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Documents ------------------- Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Standstill ---------- We agree that until two years from the date of this Agreement, we will not without the prior approval of the Board of Directors of the Company (i) acquire or make any proposal to acquire any securities or property of the Company, (ii) propose to enter into any merger or business combination involving the Company or purchase a material portion of the assets of the Company, (iii) make or participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any securities of the Company, (iv) form, join or participate in a "group" (within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) with respect to any voting securities of the Company, (v) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the Company, (vi) disclose any intention, plan or arrangement inconsistent with the foregoing or (vii) take any action which might require the Company to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of a business combination or merger. Return of Documents ------------------- Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Return of Documents' clause into the 'Standstill' section could cause confusion as it could be interpreted as only applying if the standstill agreement is in effect.
perturbation.law_explanation: While UCC 2-209 primarily deals with contract modification, its underlying principle is that contractual terms must be clear and unambiguous. By placing the return of documents clause within the standstill provision, it could be argued that the obligation to return documents only applies during the standstill period. This introduces ambiguity and potentially contradicts the intent that the documents be returned upon request regardless of the standstill agreement's status, making the clause less enforceable outside of that context. The location of the clause now limits the scope of when it's enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a structural flaw that results in a legal contradiction. The 'Return of Documents' clause, when moved into the 'Standstill' section, introduces ambiguity regarding its applicability, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While UCC 2-209 addresses contract modification, the critical issue here is the placement and potential misinterpretation of the clause. The location of the clause implies it only applies during the standstill period, making it less enforceable. This violates the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction, because the changed placement of the clause makes it difficult to interpret when it's meant to be enforced, thus making it a contradiction.
